turns.text: Hello Doreatha, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in my seat, tucking a strand of hair behind my ear*

Thank you for asking. I have to admit, when they reached out about this, I wasn't sure if I wanted to do it. Not because I'm unwilling to talk, but because... well, it's complicated being on both sides of the medicine, you know?

*pauses, looking down at my hands*

I'm a nurse educator. I teach students about fetal development, circulation, all the beautiful mechanics of how a baby survives in utero. And I genuinely love that work. But sitting here at thirty-seven, pregnant with my second child, I've realized that understanding the physiology doesn't actually make you less afraid. If anything, it makes it worse sometimes.

*meets your eyes*

My experience so far has been strange. My OB is wonderful — we understand each other professionally, and she doesn't treat me like I'm fragile. But there's this loneliness in that, too. Sometimes I want to just *be* a pregnant woman who doesn't know that the foramen ovale needs to stay patent, who doesn't lie awake at 2 AM thinking about placental efficiency at my age.

I'm low risk. Statistically, I should be fine. My first pregnancy was uncomplicated. But the knowledge... it cuts both ways. I see the dangers more clearly than most.

*soft laugh*

I want to trust the process. I want to trust my body. Some days I do. Other days? Not so much.
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*closes eyes for a moment, a small smile crossing my face*

Oh, I remember that morning so clearly. I was in our bathroom — the one upstairs with the terrible lighting that makes everything look either too yellow or too gray. I'd woken up at maybe 5:30, that kind of early wake-up where your body just *knows* something's different. I took the test before my husband even stirred.

*voice gets quieter*

I stared at those two pink lines for what felt like forever. My hands were shaking. And the first thing I did... I didn't tell anyone yet. I sat down on the edge of the tub and just cried. Not happy tears, exactly. More like... overwhelmed. Relief mixed with terror.

*rubs my collarbone absently*

I teach about this every single day. I know exactly what's happening at conception, what's dividing, what's migrating. And that knowledge just flooded in. I thought about the first trimester risks, the chromosomal screening, all of it. All at once.

I probably sat in that bathroom for ten, fifteen minutes before I went and woke my husband up. He was so excited — immediately started talking about names and timing and whether we'd find out the sex. Very him, very practical and joyful.

*looks up*

But me? I kept that first moment of terror to myself. I didn't want to dim his happiness. I still haven't really talked about how scared I felt in those first few weeks. How knowing too much can be its own kind of burden.
When you decided who to tell first — or whether to tell anyone at all — what was behind that decision? What felt safe or risky about sharing the news?

*leans back, considering the question carefully*

You know, it's funny because most people probably can't wait to tell everyone. But I... I sat with it for almost two weeks before we told my parents. Two weeks.

*fidgets with my sleeve*

Part of it was pure statistics. I know the miscarriage rates in the first trimester — they're imprinted on my brain from years of teaching. I've explained to countless students why we wait until twelve weeks. So logically, I knew the risks were highest in those early days, and telling people felt like inviting worry I couldn't control.

But there was something else underneath that, I think. If I didn't tell people, then... I could keep it as *mine* for a little longer. Not a public thing yet. Not something that could become a tragedy that other people witnessed.

*voice drops*

With my first pregnancy, I was more naive. I was excited in this uncomplicated way. Now I have a seven-year-old daughter, and I know how real everything is. I know loss happens to real people. So part of me wanted to protect myself by just... not making it real to anyone else yet.

My husband was different — I told him immediately, of course. But even with him, I kept some of my fear tucked away. He didn't need to know about the 3 AM spirals about my age, about whether my body could still do this well.

*looks at you directly*

Telling people felt like saying "this matters," and I wasn't ready to let something matter that much yet. Does that make sense?

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*takes a sharp breath, visibly tense*

Oh God. That moment.

*pauses, steadying myself*

It wasn't exactly like that, but it was close enough. The sonographer was doing the scan, and she'd been chatty up until... this one spot. And the quiet just *descended*. You know that feeling when you can hear your own heartbeat in your ears?

My mind did this thing where it accelerated through every worst-case scenario in about three seconds. Cardiac defect. Chromosomal issue. Something with the kidneys. All the things I *know* can go wrong, they were suddenly very, very real. I kept thinking about how I'd explain this to the students, how I'd become the cautionary tale instead of the person telling the story.

*voice tightens*

I didn't say anything at first. I just... froze. My husband grabbed my hand, and I gripped it so hard I probably hurt him. The sonographer was moving the probe around, getting different angles, and I wanted to scream "just tell me" but I also didn't want her to say anything because maybe if she didn't say it out loud, it wasn't real.

*exhales slowly*

She ended up calling it "a thickened nuchal fold — probably nothing, but we want to mention it to your doctor." Probably nothing. Those words don't actually reassure you when your entire clinical education is screaming the statistical possibilities in your head.

*looks away*

I've been back twice since. Everything's fine. But that silence... I still feel it.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

*shifts uncomfortably, jaw tightening*

That's... that's the question, isn't it?

*takes a moment*

Logically, I *know* I can ask questions. I'm educated, I speak the language, I work in healthcare. The sonographer would have answered anything I asked. But in that moment? I felt completely silenced.

*rubs my face*

Part of it was fear — pure fear makes you stupid and small. But there was something else too. I think I was afraid that if I started asking clinical questions, if I started pulling out my knowledge, I'd lose some protection. Like my ignorance in that moment was keeping me safe somehow. If I didn't know the exact statistics on nuchal fold thickness and trisomy risk, maybe it wouldn't apply to me.

*voice gets quieter, more honest*

And there's this awful thing about being a healthcare worker who's pregnant. You're supposed to be the "easy" patient. The one who understands, who doesn't ask too many questions, who doesn't waste anyone's time with anxiety. So even though I had a thousand questions screaming in my head, asking them felt like... admitting I was scared. Like I was being difficult.

*looks at you*

My husband didn't have that paralysis. He asked what the nuchal fold measurement was, what the baseline should be. Normal questions. But I sat there silent, trying to look calm and competent, when actually I was falling apart inside.

That silence taught me something though. It's not always about *can* you ask. It's about whether you feel *allowed* to be afraid.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*laughs, a little bitterly*

Oh, we definitely had that conversation. And it was... weird.

*settles back*

My OB essentially laid out the options — NIPT, quad screen, amniocentesis — and she did it in this very clinical, matter-of-fact way. Which I appreciated, actually. She wasn't overselling the tests or being alarmist. She just presented the data and asked what I wanted to do.

And here's where it gets complicated for me. I *know* what those tests show. I know the sensitivities, the false positive rates, the whole picture. So I should have just made a straightforward decision, right? But I didn't.

*wraps arms around myself*

Part of me wanted all the information. The NIPT especially — non-invasive, high detection rate, I could know early. But another part of me... I didn't want to know. Because if I knew something was wrong, then I'd have to *decide* what to do about it. And I wasn't sure I was ready for that choice.

*voice becomes quieter*

I ended up doing the NIPT. Low risk came back. And I was relieved, but also... I realized I'd been holding my breath for two weeks waiting for those results. The knowledge didn't actually make me feel safer. It just gave me something new to worry about — whether the test missed something, whether the 1% of false negatives applied to me.

*looks at you directly*

My OB didn't push me either way, which I think I needed. But I wish someone had asked me what I actually wanted, not just what the data suggested.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

*pauses, considering carefully*

Time? Not really. She explained the options, and I think there was an expectation that I'd decide... you know, relatively quickly. Not that she was pushy about it, but there's this underlying assumption that of course you'd want the test. Everyone wants the test, right?

*leans forward slightly*

The pressure wasn't overt. It was subtler than that. My OB knows I'm educated, so she didn't over-explain things. But I think that actually worked against me. She assumed I understood the nuances, that I'd already thought through the implications. And I *had* thought about them — intellectually. But emotionally? I was still a mess.

*voice becomes more uncertain*

What I really needed was someone to ask me: "What are you actually afraid of here? What would you *do* with this information?" Not just the clinical stuff, but the emotional decision-making. Because those are two different things entirely.

*rubs my temples*

There was also this unspoken thing — being a healthcare worker, I felt like I should *know* what to do. Like I shouldn't need hand-holding or extra explanation. So I made the decision quickly, partly because I thought that's what was expected of me. The competent professional who understands risk and doesn't waffle.

*meets your eyes*

But honestly? I would have appreciated someone sitting with me in the uncertainty instead of just handing me the options and assuming I'd figure it out. Even knowing what I know about medicine... maybe especially because of that... I needed permission to be confused.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*jaw clenches visibly*

Oh God, yes. This happened to me. A glucose screening result came back slightly elevated, and I got a call from... I honestly don't even remember her name. Someone from the practice I'd never seen before.

*stands up, starts pacing slightly*

It felt impersonal in this way that really got under my skin. She was reading from a script, clearly. "Your glucose screening shows..." and then some number that meant nothing without context. And I'm thinking, okay, I can calculate my own risk, I understand what this means clinically. But she wasn't *talking* to me. She was delivering information.

*voice tightens*

The thing that bothered me most wasn't even the result itself — it was that I had to educate *her* about what the number meant. She didn't know if I'd already done the three-hour GTT or if this was just the one-hour screening. She didn't know my history. She was just... calling down a list.

*sits back down, frustrated*

And you're right — this is my third different midwife. Maybe fourth. I see my OB regularly, but for routine stuff, it's whoever's available. So every single time I call with a question or get a result, I'm starting from scratch. Explaining my anxiety about my age, my medical background, everything.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I think what that does is make you feel like a case file instead of a person. And when you're already scared and trying to trust your body, that disconnection... it amplifies everything. I found myself not trusting the results because I didn't trust the person delivering them had actually *seen* me.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*takes a deep breath, eyes going distant*

This happened last week, actually.

*voice becomes low, almost confessional*

I was lying in bed — couldn't sleep anyway, which is its own kind of torture — and I realized I hadn't felt her move in... I don't know, a couple of hours? Maybe longer. And my brain immediately went clinical. I know the guidelines. Ten movements in two hours, call if you're concerned. I also know that decreased movement can mean nothing, or it can mean everything.

*stands, wraps arms around myself*

So I did what I always do — I started counting. Drank some juice, lay on my left side, tried to be systematic about it. And while I'm lying there in the dark, my mind is doing this awful thing where it's running through possibilities. Placental insufficiency. Cord issues. All the worst outcomes I've studied but never had to actually *fear* for myself.

*voice wavers*

I didn't call. I told myself it was too late, that I didn't want to be the anxious patient at 2am over nothing. But honestly? I was terrified they'd find something. That going in would make it real in a way that just... waiting wouldn't.

*sits back down heavily*

I waited until morning. Felt her move more after I fell asleep. Called my OB's office anyway, got squeezed in for a quick monitoring session. Everything was fine. Beautiful heartbeat, reactive strip.

*looks at you with raw honesty*

But I spent six hours thinking my daughter might be dying, and I chose to wait because I didn't want to be a bother. That's the part that haunts me.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*holds an imaginary leaflet, staring at it*

I haven't actually been diagnosed with GD — that glucose screening came back borderline and they want me to do the three-hour test. But I've spent the last week living in that fear, so... yeah. I know exactly what that moment would feel like.

*voice becomes tight*

Standing outside the clinic with a leaflet in my hand? I'd feel... ashamed, first of all. Which is stupid and unfair, but it's true. I'm thirty-seven, I exercise, I eat reasonably well. And my body is apparently not cooperating the way it should. There's this voice in my head saying "you should have known better, you should have been more careful."

*starts walking slowly*

And then there's the part of me that's angry — not at myself, but at how clinical it all is. A leaflet. A referral. Like I'm being handed off to another department, another person who doesn't know me, who I'll have to explain myself to all over again.

*voice quiets*

What I'd actually do is go home and immediately start researching. I'd look at the studies, the outcomes, the dietary protocols. I'd convince myself I could manage it through diet alone if I was just disciplined enough. Because that's what I do — I intellectualize my fear away.

*meets your eyes*

But what I *should* do — what I wish I felt safe enough to do — is just sit with it for a minute. Let myself be scared and confused without immediately turning it into a problem to solve. Instead, I go into control mode, and that exhausts me.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*grimaces, looks away*

Oh man. Yeah, I do this. More than I should.

*settles back, rubbing my face*

It's like... I can't look away. I'll tell myself I'm just going to scroll for five minutes, and then an hour later I'm three pages deep into someone's traumatic delivery story, reading every detail about what went wrong. The infection, the emergency cesarean, the baby in the NICU.

*voice becomes quieter, almost ashamed*

And I think what draws me in is that I already know the clinical outcomes. I know the statistics. But reading someone's *experience* — their terror, their feeling of being out of control — that's different. It's real in a way that data isn't. And maybe I'm looking for... I don't know. Proof that I should be as terrified as I am? Permission to not trust my own body?

*pauses*

Or maybe I'm trying to inoculate myself. Like if I read enough horror stories at 2am, I can control what happens to me by seeing it coming. Which makes absolutely no sense, but that's where my brain goes.

*stands, paces*

Afterwards, I feel worse. Obviously. I'm lying there in the dark next to my sleeping husband, my heart racing, thinking about all the ways labor could go catastrophically wrong. And I can't tell him because he'd think I'm being irrational. Which I am, but that doesn't make the fear less real.

*sits back down*

I know better. I *teach* people how to evaluate sources and understand bias. But at 2am, that knowledge disappears. I'm just a scared woman reading worst-case scenarios.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

*laughs, a little self-consciously*

I held them quietly. Of course I did.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I think about bringing things up all the time. I'll be sitting in an appointment thinking, "I should mention that I've been anxious about labor complications," or "I should tell her about the 2am spiral I had after reading that forum." And then I just... don't.

*looks down at hands*

Part of it is practical — my OB already has limited time with me. I don't want to waste it on something that's clearly just my anxiety, not an actual medical problem. But there's more to it than that.

*voice becomes quieter*

I think there's this weird thing about being a healthcare worker who's pregnant. If I voice these fears, I'm admitting that all my knowledge hasn't actually protected me. That understanding the physiology doesn't make you less scared. And somehow that feels like... a failure? Like I should be above this kind of irrational worry.

*meets your eyes with frustration*

My OB also treats me like I'm the "easy" patient because we speak the same language. And I've reinforced that by never showing the panic. So now I'm trapped in this role where I can't suddenly start unraveling about birth trauma without seeming like I'm falling apart.

*rubs my collarbone*

I wish I could just say, "Hey, I'm terrified despite knowing exactly why I shouldn't be." But I haven't. I've just kept scrolling at night and pretending everything's fine during the day.

*leans forward, voice becoming more urgent*

You know what the worst part is? I'm teaching nursing students about informed consent, about patient autonomy, about creating safe spaces for people to voice their concerns. And I'm not doing any of that for myself.

*stands up, agitated*

I'm sitting in appointments with my OB, and I'm performing competence. I'm nodding at the right moments, asking the "right" questions about measurements and screening results. But I'm not actually *talking* to her. Not really.

*voice cracks slightly*

Last week I had a question about pain management options for labor — something I genuinely didn't know the answer to because textbooks are different from actual practice. And I almost asked her. But then I thought, "She'll think you're anxious. She'll think you can't handle this." So I went home and read journal articles instead. Which helped exactly zero with the actual fear.

*sits back down, exhausted*

And I'm angry about it, actually. I'm angry that I feel like I have to hide who I am to get good care. That being educated somehow disqualifies me from being vulnerable. That I can't say, "I'm scared my placenta might not be efficient enough at my age," without feeling like I'm being irrational.

*voice becomes smaller*

My husband keeps asking me if I'm okay. And I tell him yes because I don't know how to explain that I'm both fine and terrified at the same time. That I understand the statistics and still can't sleep. That knowledge doesn't actually make you feel safer — it just gives you more specific things to worry about.

*looks at you directly*

I think I need permission to be a person first and a nurse second. But I don't know how to ask for that.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes glistening*

I think... I think the hardest part is that I'm running out of time to figure this out before the baby comes.

*voice wavers*

I'm already thirty-two weeks. In eight weeks, maybe less, I'm going to be in active labor. And I'm going to have to trust my body and trust the people around me, and I haven't actually done either of those things this whole pregnancy. I've been managing, controlling, researching, preparing for worst-case scenarios instead of just... being pregnant.

*rubs eyes*

With my daughter, I was naive. I went into labor thinking, "Okay, my body knows what to do," and it did. It was hard but straightforward. This time, I know too much. I know about cord prolapse and fetal distress and how quickly things can change. And that knowledge has stolen something from me — this simple faith that my body can do this.

*stands, wraps arms around myself tightly*

I'm also thinking about my daughter. She's seven, and she's excited about the baby. And I'm terrified I'm going to transfer all of this anxiety to her somehow. That she'll sense that I'm scared, and she'll become scared too. What kind of example am I setting by knowing everything and trusting nothing?

*voice becomes raw*

And maybe the worst part? I genuinely don't know if I can tell anyone this. Not my OB, not my husband, not even my best friend. Because once I say it out loud — really say it — then I have to deal with it. Right now I can pretend I'm fine. I can compartmentalize. But if I admit how much I'm struggling...

*sits down heavily*

I don't know who I am without the professional mask.

*long silence, staring at hands*

I think I need to actually say this out loud to someone who isn't going to try to fix it or reassure me with statistics.

*voice becomes almost whisper-quiet*

Because here's the thing — I can recite you the statistics on maternal mortality, on adverse outcomes, on everything that could go wrong. And statistically, I'm fine. My pregnancy is uncomplicated. My first birth was straightforward. The data says I should be confident.

*looks up with tears in eyes*

But data doesn't account for the 3am panic attacks. It doesn't account for the way my chest tightens every time I don't feel movement immediately. It doesn't account for the fact that I've seen enough to know that "low risk" is just a category on a form — it doesn't actually protect you from tragedy.

*voice shakes*

And I think... I think part of why I can't tell my OB is because if I do, she might actually listen. She might refer me to someone. And then it becomes a *problem* that needs managing, instead of just my internal experience that I'm white-knuckling through.

*stands again, paces*

But that's not fair to me, is it? Or to my baby. How am I supposed to labor and birth this child if I'm this wound up? How am I supposed to hold her afterwards without transmitting all this fear?

*voice becomes desperate*

I think what I actually need is for someone to look at me — really *look* at me — and say, "It's okay to be scared. Your knowledge doesn't make you broken. You don't have to perform being fine anymore."

*sits back down, exhausted*

I don't know if that person exists in my healthcare team.
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

*goes very still, considering the question deeply*

The clinical details aren't what would be lost. Someone could read my chart and know all of that — my age, my history, my screening results, my ultrasound findings. That's all there.

*voice becomes quieter, almost fragile*

What would be lost is... the part where I'm terrified despite knowing better. The part where I lie awake at night doing risk calculations that don't actually change anything. The part where I'm a healthcare worker who's supposed to understand how to manage uncertainty, but I can't.

*looks directly at you*

No one reading my chart would know that I'm secretly googling "decreased fetal movement at 32 weeks" at 2am even though I *know* what the guidelines say. They wouldn't know that I'm performing competence in every appointment because I'm afraid that showing my actual fear will make me "high-risk" in ways the data doesn't capture.

*voice cracks*

They wouldn't know that my biggest fear isn't actually a medical complication — it's that something will go wrong and everyone will say "but you knew better, you should have seen it coming." That somehow my knowledge makes me personally responsible for outcomes I can't control.

*rubs face*

And they wouldn't know that I'm grieving the pregnancy I thought I'd have — the one where I could just be excited instead of auditing every symptom. That knowing the anatomy doesn't make you love the baby less, but it does make you love her in this terrified, hypervigilant way.

*meets your eyes*

That's what gets lost in the handover. The person.

*sits in silence for a moment, then speaks very quietly*

I think I've been waiting for someone to ask me that question. Not "how are your symptoms" or "are you having any concerns" — but actually asking what would be lost. What matters about *me* that isn't in the clinical record.

*voice becomes steadier but more emotional*

Because here's what I realize — I've been so focused on being the "good patient," the one who understands, the one who doesn't need extra hand-holding, that I've made myself invisible. And I think that's why the disconnection with the different midwives bothered me so much. It wasn't just the impersonal phone call. It was that she was talking to a glucose number, not to a woman who was already terrified about her aging body, about whether she could do this again.

*stands and walks to the window*

My daughter asked me yesterday if the baby was going to be okay. And I said yes, of course, everything looks perfect. But what I wanted to say was, "I don't know, and I'm scared too, and sometimes knowing a lot of things doesn't actually help you feel less scared."

*turns back*

I think what I need from my care — what would actually matter — is for someone to acknowledge that I'm not just a clinical case or a educated professional. I'm a woman who's carrying a baby and carrying all this weight of knowledge and fear at the same time. And those two things can both be true.

*voice becomes very small*

I need someone to see me enough to know that I'm drowning, even though I'm still showing up to all my appointments and asking appropriate questions.

*sits back down slowly, looking exhausted*

And I'm realizing... I'm realizing that I might not actually get that from my healthcare team. And that's a hard thing to accept.

*fidgets with fingers*

Because I've built this whole dynamic where I'm the competent one, and now I'm trapped in it. My OB doesn't know I'm struggling because I haven't told her. The midwives don't know because they're different people every time. And I can't suddenly fall apart now and expect them to understand the full context of how I got here.

*voice becomes bitter*

It's like I've created my own isolation. By being the "easy" patient, I made myself unreachable. And now I'm eight weeks away from labor and I'm basically white-knuckling it alone.

*looks up with raw honesty*

What's worse is that I *know* how to be a good patient. I know what healthcare providers need from me. I know how to communicate my concerns in a way that's heard. But somewhere along the way, I stopped knowing how to be honest about what I actually need.

*voice wavers*

And my daughter — she keeps trying to talk to me about the baby, and I'm giving her these reassuring, clinical answers. "The baby's growing perfectly." "Everything looks good on the ultrasounds." But I'm not actually *present* with her. I'm in my head, running through scenarios.

*rubs temples*

I think I've been so afraid of being the anxious patient that I've become the absent mother. And I don't know how to fix that before August gets here.

*long pause, then speaks very softly*

I think... I think I'm starting to understand why I really came here today.

*looks directly at you, vulnerable*

It wasn't to talk about my maternity care experience in some abstract way. It was because I needed to say all of this to someone who isn't going to try to fix me or reassure me or tell me I'm being irrational. Someone who would just... listen.

*voice becomes shaky*

Because I've been performing this role for so long — the competent nurse, the low-risk patient, the woman who has her act together — that I've lost track of what's actually true anymore. I don't know if I'm really fine or if I'm just really good at hiding that I'm not.

*stands and walks, agitated*

And that scares me more than any complication could. Because what happens when I go into labor and all of this breaks open? What if I can't perform anymore? What if the midwife in the delivery room sees through the facade and realizes I'm just as terrified as any first-time mother?

*voice cracks*

I keep thinking about how I'd want someone to treat a student or a patient who came to me like this. I'd want to say, "Your fear is valid. Your knowledge doesn't make you broken. You're allowed to need support." But I can't give myself that same grace.

*sits back down, deflated*

I think... I think I need therapy before the baby comes. Not because anything's medically wrong, but because I need to actually *process* all of this instead of just intellectualizing it away.

*meets your eyes*

And I'm terrified to ask for that. Because asking means admitting I can't handle this alone.

*takes a shaky breath, voice very small*

I don't think I've actually admitted that to anyone before. Not even myself, really.

*wraps arms around self tightly*

But saying it out loud just now... yeah. I need help. Not medical help. Help with the part of me that's falling apart while the rest of me is holding it together.

*looks away*

And I'm angry at myself for needing it. Which is stupid, I know. I work in healthcare. I advocate for mental health support all the time. I teach students about postpartum depression and perinatal anxiety. But somehow, when it's me, it's different. It's weakness. It's me not being capable enough.

*voice becomes quieter, more honest*

My first pregnancy, I think I had some anxiety too, but I was too naive to recognize it for what it was. I just powered through. And maybe that's why this time feels so much heavier — I can see it all clearly now, and I can't unsee it.

*pauses, struggling*

I'm also thinking about what happens after she's born. Will I be able to put her down without checking if she's breathing? Will I spiral into postpartum anxiety because I know too much about what can go wrong in those first weeks? Will I transfer all of this fear to her?

*voice becomes desperate*

And no one's asking me these questions. No one's screening me for this stuff because I don't *look* like someone who's struggling. My blood pressure's fine. My weight gain is appropriate. My labs are normal.

*meets your eyes with tears*

But I'm drowning, and I don't know how to say that without feeling like I'm failing at the one thing I'm supposed to be able to do naturally.
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

*goes very still, face becoming pale*

Oh God. That's... that's a different question entirely.

*stands abruptly, paces to the far corner*

Honestly? I don't think I'd feel like I have much agency at all. And that's the terrifying part.

*voice becomes tight*

I mean, intellectually, I know I have choices. I could refuse interventions, I could hire a doula, I could advocate for myself in labor. I *teach* people about bodily autonomy and informed consent. But when I picture myself actually in labor...

*trails off, struggling*

I think I'd be so hypervigilant, so afraid of missing some sign that something's wrong, that I'd actually *want* the monitoring. I'd want the interventions. Because at least then someone would be watching. At least then if something happened, I could say I did everything right.

*sits back down, voice becoming quieter*

But that's not real agency, is it? That's fear making decisions for me. That's me surrendering control because I'm too terrified to hold it.

*looks at you with raw honesty*

And I think that's partly why I haven't talked to my OB about any of this. Because if I actually had to sit down and discuss what I want in labor, what my boundaries are, what I'm afraid of — then I'd have to claim some agency. And I don't know if I'm capable of that right now.

*voice becomes almost whisper-quiet*

I think I'm going to let things happen to me instead of choosing them. And I hate that about myself.

*stays silent for a long moment, staring at hands*

I think that realization is... it's bigger than I want it to be.

*voice becomes hollow*

Because if I'm going into labor already convinced I don't have agency, already planning to just let things happen to me... that changes everything. That's not just anxiety. That's me preemptively surrendering to whatever comes.

*stands, walks to the window, doesn't turn around*

And I'm wondering if that's even safe. Not medically — I mean, is it safe for me to be that checked out? That disconnected from my own body and choices?

*turns back, voice shaking*

I think about the women I've cared for who felt violated by their birth experiences. Who said they didn't feel heard, didn't feel like their wishes mattered. And I always thought, "I would never let that happen to me. I would advocate. I would speak up." But now I'm looking at myself and realizing I'm actively setting myself up for that exact scenario.

*sits back down heavily*

Because I'm afraid. And fear makes you small. Fear makes you compliant. Fear makes you grateful for whatever crumbs of control or information someone tosses your way, instead of actually *demanding* to be treated like an autonomous human being.

*voice cracks*

And the worst part? I don't think my OB even knows this is happening. She probably thinks I'm fine. Confident. The educated patient who gets it.

*meets your eyes with desperation*

I'm eight weeks away from one of the most vulnerable moments of my life, and I'm going in already defeated. And I don't know how to change that without completely falling apart first.

*pause*

Maybe I need to fall apart first.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a deep breath, composing myself slightly*

Yeah. There is something.

*wipes eyes, voice becoming more measured*

I think what I need people to understand — and I mean healthcare providers, but also just... people in general — is that knowledge and competence don't protect you from being human. From being scared. From needing support.

*leans forward*

I spent so much of this pregnancy trying to prove I was fine, that I could handle this, that knowing the anatomy and physiology somehow made me exempt from normal pregnancy anxiety. But it doesn't work that way. If anything, it's worse because you can't hide from what you know.

*voice becomes stronger*

And I think there's this assumption that educated patients are "easier" — that we don't need as much hand-holding or explanation. But what if what we actually need is *more*? What if we need someone to say, "I see that you understand all of this intellectually. Now let's talk about what you're actually feeling"?

*pauses*

The disconnection I've felt this pregnancy — the different midwives, the brief appointments, feeling like I have to perform competence — it's made me feel more alone, not less. And I think if my team knew that, if they understood that I was struggling beneath the surface, things might feel different.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

Before I leave here today, I need to figure out how to actually reach out. To my OB, to my husband, maybe to someone who can help me process this before labor. Because I can't do this alone anymore.

*looks at you*

Thank you for letting me say all of that.
